kids.question: Who wrote this letter?
kids.option_a: Paul
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:1 — "Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God."

kids.question: What does God do for us when we are in trouble?
kids.option_a: He comforts us
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:4 — "Who comforteth us in all our tribulation."

kids.question: Who was with Paul when he wrote this letter?
kids.option_a: Timothy
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:1 — "Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, and Timothy our brother."

teens.question: What title does Paul give God in verse 3?
teens.option_a: The Father of mercies and the God of all comfort
teens.option_b: The God of thunder and lightning
teens.option_c: The Lord of armies and battles
teens.option_d: The King of all earthly kingdoms
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:3 — "Blessed be God, even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies, and the God of all comfort."

teens.question: Why does God comfort us in our tribulation?
teens.option_a: That we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble, by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God
teens.option_b: So we can boast about our strength
teens.option_c: So we can avoid all future suffering
teens.option_d: So we never have to help anyone else
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:4 — "Who comforteth us in all our tribulation, that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble, by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God."

teens.question: What happened to Paul in Asia?
teens.option_a: He was pressed out of measure, above strength, insomuch that he despaired even of life
teens.option_b: He built a great church with many members
teens.option_c: He found a treasure of gold and silver
teens.option_d: He was crowned king of the province
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:8 — "For we would not, brethren, have you ignorant of our trouble which came to us in Asia, that we were pressed out of measure, above strength, insomuch that we despaired even of life."

teens.question: Why did Paul receive the sentence of death in himself?
teens.option_a: That he should not trust in himself but in God which raiseth the dead
teens.option_b: To make him give up his ministry
teens.option_c: To punish him for past sins
teens.option_d: To test his physical endurance
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:9 — "But we had the sentence of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves, but in God which raiseth the dead."

teens.question: What has God given as an earnest (guarantee) in our hearts?
teens.option_a: The Spirit — God hath sealed us and given the earnest of the Spirit in our hearts
teens.option_b: A written contract of salvation
teens.option_c: Gold and silver as a deposit
teens.option_d: A promise from the angels
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:22 — "Who hath also sealed us, and given the earnest of the Spirit in our hearts."

adults.question: How does Paul describe the relationship between Christ's sufferings and comfort?
adults.option_a: As the sufferings of Christ abound in us, so our consolation also aboundeth by Christ
adults.option_b: Suffering and comfort are unrelated experiences
adults.option_c: Comfort comes only after all suffering has ended
adults.option_d: Christ's sufferings eliminate the need for human suffering
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:5 — "For as the sufferings of Christ abound in us, so our consolation also aboundeth by Christ."

adults.question: What does Paul say about all the promises of God?
adults.option_a: In him they are yea, and in him Amen, unto the glory of God by us
adults.option_b: They are conditional upon perfect obedience
adults.option_c: They apply only to the apostles
adults.option_d: They are symbolic and not literal
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:20 — "For all the promises of God in him are yea, and in him Amen, unto the glory of God by us."

adults.question: Who does Paul say establishes believers in Christ and has anointed them?
adults.option_a: God — who also hath sealed us and given the earnest of the Spirit
adults.option_b: The apostles through their teaching
adults.option_c: The law of Moses through obedience
adults.option_d: The church through baptism
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:21-22 — "Now he which stablisheth us with you in Christ, and hath anointed us, is God; Who hath also sealed us, and given the earnest of the Spirit in our hearts."

adults.question: Why did Paul call God as a witness upon his soul?
adults.option_a: To spare them he came not as yet unto Corinth — not because he had dominion over their faith but was a helper of their joy
adults.option_b: Because he was afraid of the journey
adults.option_c: Because he wanted to visit Rome first
adults.option_d: Because the Corinthians owed him money
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:23-24 — "Moreover I call God for a record upon my soul, that to spare you I came not as yet unto Corinth. Not for that we have dominion over your faith, but are helpers of your joy."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the Corinthians' standing in faith?
adults.option_a: By faith ye stand — he is not lord over their faith but a helper of their joy
adults.option_b: They are weak and falling away
adults.option_c: They need Paul to sustain their faith
adults.option_d: They have no faith of their own
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:24 — "Not for that we have dominion over your faith, but are helpers of your joy: for by faith ye stand."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by saying he had the "sentence of death" in himself?
adults.option_a: He had given up hope of survival humanly speaking, so that he would learn to trust only in God who raises the dead
adults.option_b: He was formally sentenced to execution by a Roman court
adults.option_c: He was suffering from a terminal illness
adults.option_d: He was feeling depressed about his ministry
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:9 — "But we had the sentence of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves, but in God which raiseth the dead."

adults.question: What role does Paul say the Corinthians play in his deliverance?
adults.option_a: They help together by prayer for him, so that thanks may be given by many persons on his behalf
adults.option_b: They send him financial support
adults.option_c: They send bodyguards to protect him
adults.option_d: They write letters to the Roman authorities
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:11 — "Ye also helping together by prayer for us, that for the gift bestowed upon us by the means of many persons thanks may be given by many on our behalf."

adults.question: What does Paul say his rejoicing and the testimony of his conscience is?
adults.option_a: That in simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom but by the grace of God, he had his conversation in the world
adults.option_b: That he performed many miracles
adults.option_c: That he converted the most people
adults.option_d: That he had the most theological knowledge
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 1:12 — "For our rejoicing is this, the testimony of our conscience, that in simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom, but by the grace of God, we have had our conversation in the world, and more abundantly to you-ward."

kids.question: What did Paul write to the Corinthians with?
kids.option_a: Many tears and a loving heart
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:4 — "For out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote unto you with many tears."

kids.question: What should we do when someone is sorry for what they did wrong?
kids.option_a: Forgive them and comfort them
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:7 — "So that contrariwise ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him, lest perhaps such a one should be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow."

kids.question: What does God always cause us to do in Christ?
kids.option_a: Triumph (win)
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:14 — "Now thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ."

teens.question: Why did Paul write with tears instead of visiting?
teens.option_a: He did not want to come again to them in heaviness, but wrote that his joy might be theirs
teens.option_b: He was too busy with other churches
teens.option_c: He was forbidden by the authorities
teens.option_d: He had lost interest in the Corinthians
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:1,3 — "But I determined this with myself, that I would not come again to you in heaviness... that my joy is the joy of you all."

teens.question: Why should the Corinthians forgive the offender?
teens.option_a: Lest Satan should get an advantage of them, for we are not ignorant of his devices
teens.option_b: Because the offender was an important person
teens.option_c: Because Paul would punish them if they didn't
teens.option_d: Because forgiveness earns heavenly rewards
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:10-11 — "Lest Satan should get an advantage of us: for we are not ignorant of his devices."

teens.question: What does Paul call the knowledge of Christ?
teens.option_a: A sweet savour — God maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge by us in every place
teens.option_b: A heavy burden to carry
teens.option_c: A mystery no one can understand
teens.option_d: A secret for apostles only
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:14 — "Now thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ, and maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge by us in every place."

teens.question: What is the gospel to those who are saved versus those who perish?
teens.option_a: To one a savour of death unto death, and to the other a savour of life unto life
teens.option_b: The same pleasant message to everyone
teens.option_c: Confusing to both groups equally
teens.option_d: Only meaningful to educated people
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:15-16 — "For we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ, in them that are saved, and in them that perish: To the one we are the savour of death unto death; and to the other the savour of life unto life."

teens.question: What does Paul say they are NOT doing with the word of God?
teens.option_a: They are not as many, which corrupt the word of God, but speak in sincerity as of God in the sight of God
teens.option_b: They are not reading it enough
teens.option_c: They are not translating it properly
teens.option_d: They are not memorizing it daily
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:17 — "For we are not as many, which corrupt the word of God: but as of sincerity, but as of God, in the sight of God speak we in Christ."

adults.question: What was the punishment inflicted on the offender by many?
adults.option_a: Sufficient punishment — Paul now urges forgiveness and comfort lest he be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow
adults.option_b: A fine of money paid to the church
adults.option_c: Permanent exile from the city
adults.option_d: A public flogging in the synagogue
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:6-7 — "Sufficient to such a man is this punishment, which was inflicted of many. So that contrariwise ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him, lest perhaps such a one should be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow."

adults.question: What does Paul urge the Corinthians to confirm toward the offender?
adults.option_a: Their love — that he might know the proof of them, whether they be obedient in all things
adults.option_b: Their authority over him
adults.option_c: Their superiority to him
adults.option_d: Their indifference toward him
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:8-9 — "Wherefore I beseech you that ye would confirm your love toward him. For to this end also did I write, that I might know the proof of you, whether ye be obedient in all things."

adults.question: In whose person does Paul say he forgave?
adults.option_a: In the person of Christ — what he forgave, he forgave for their sakes in the person of Christ
adults.option_b: In the person of Moses
adults.option_c: In the person of the high priest
adults.option_d: In his own person as an apostle
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:10 — "To whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also: for if I forgave any thing, to whom I forgave it, for your sakes forgave I it in the person of Christ."

adults.question: What happened when Paul came to Troas to preach?
adults.option_a: A door was opened unto him of the Lord, but he had no rest in his spirit because he found not Titus
adults.option_b: He was arrested and imprisoned
adults.option_c: He performed many miracles
adults.option_d: He found a large congregation waiting
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:12-13 — "Furthermore, when I came to Troas to preach Christ's gospel, and a door was opened unto me of the Lord, I had no rest in my spirit, because I found not Titus my brother."

adults.question: What profound question does Paul ask in verse 16?
adults.option_a: Who is sufficient for these things? — referring to the weighty responsibility of being a savour of life or death
adults.option_b: How many churches should one apostle oversee?
adults.option_c: When will Christ return to earth?
adults.option_d: Where should the next church be planted?
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:16 — "And who is sufficient for these things?"

adults.question: What does Paul mean by those who "corrupt" the word of God?
adults.option_a: Those who peddle or adulterate God's word for profit, as opposed to speaking sincerely as from God in Christ
adults.option_b: Those who accidentally misquote Scripture
adults.option_c: Those who translate it into other languages
adults.option_d: Those who read it without understanding
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:17 — "For we are not as many, which corrupt the word of God: but as of sincerity, but as of God, in the sight of God speak we in Christ."

adults.question: Why did Paul leave Troas despite the open door for the gospel?
adults.option_a: He had no rest in his spirit because he did not find Titus, so he went into Macedonia
adults.option_b: The authorities shut down his preaching
adults.option_c: He ran out of money for the journey
adults.option_d: The church at Troas rejected his message
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:13 — "I had no rest in my spirit, because I found not Titus my brother: but taking my leave of them, I went from thence into Macedonia."

adults.question: What does Paul say about their manner of speaking in the sight of God?
adults.option_a: As of sincerity, as of God, in the sight of God speak we in Christ — transparent accountability before God
adults.option_b: They speak with great eloquence and rhetoric
adults.option_c: They speak only when inspired by visions
adults.option_d: They speak with the authority of the Sanhedrin
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:17 — "But as of sincerity, but as of God, in the sight of God speak we in Christ."

kids.question: What does Paul call the Corinthian believers?
kids.option_a: His letter, written in their hearts
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:2 — "Ye are our epistle written in our hearts, known and read of all men."

kids.question: What does the Spirit give?
kids.option_a: Life
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:6 — "The letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life."

kids.question: Where the Spirit of the Lord is, what do we have?
kids.option_a: Liberty (freedom)
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:17 — "Where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty."

teens.question: What is the Corinthians' epistle written with?
teens.option_a: Not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not in tables of stone but in fleshy tables of the heart
teens.option_b: With expensive gold ink on parchment
teens.option_c: With ordinary ink on papyrus scrolls
teens.option_d: With carved letters on marble tablets
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:3 — "Written not with ink, but with the Spirit of the living God; not in tables of stone, but in fleshy tables of the heart."

teens.question: What does the letter do versus what the spirit does?
teens.option_a: The letter killeth but the spirit giveth life
teens.option_b: The letter gives life but the spirit restricts
teens.option_c: Both the letter and spirit give life equally
teens.option_d: The letter and spirit are the same thing
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:6 — "Who also hath made us able ministers of the new testament; not of the letter, but of the spirit: for the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life."

teens.question: What happened to Moses' face when he came down from the mountain?
teens.option_a: His face shone with glory so the children of Israel could not steadfastly behold it
teens.option_b: His face turned completely white
teens.option_c: His face became invisible
teens.option_d: His face looked exactly the same as before
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:7 — "The ministration of death, written and engraven in stones, was glorious, so that the children of Israel could not stedfastly behold the face of Moses for the glory of his countenance."

teens.question: What is still over the hearts of Israel when Moses is read?
teens.option_a: A veil — the vail is upon their heart, but when they turn to the Lord, the vail shall be taken away
teens.option_b: A crown of thorns
teens.option_c: A cloud of darkness
teens.option_d: A wall of fire
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:15-16 — "But even unto this day, when Moses is read, the vail is upon their heart. Nevertheless when it shall turn to the Lord, the vail shall be taken away."

teens.question: What are believers being changed into?
teens.option_a: The same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord
teens.option_b: Powerful warriors
teens.option_c: Perfect scholars
teens.option_d: Wealthy rulers
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:18 — "But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord."

adults.question: What does Paul say about needing epistles of commendation?
adults.option_a: He needs no letters of commendation to or from them because they are his epistle, known and read of all men
adults.option_b: He requires formal letters from every church
adults.option_c: He carries letters from the Jerusalem council
adults.option_d: He insists on written approval from Peter
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:1-2 — "Do we begin again to commend ourselves? or need we, as some others, epistles of commendation to you, or letters of commendation from you? Ye are our epistle written in our hearts, known and read of all men."

adults.question: What does Paul say about his sufficiency?
adults.option_a: Not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think any thing as of ourselves; but our sufficiency is of God
adults.option_b: His sufficiency comes from his rabbinic training
adults.option_c: His sufficiency is from years of experience
adults.option_d: His sufficiency is from the Jerusalem church
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:5 — "Not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think any thing as of ourselves; but our sufficiency is of God."

adults.question: How does Paul contrast the ministry of death with the ministry of the spirit?
adults.option_a: If the ministration of death, written and engraven in stones, was glorious, the ministration of the spirit must be rather more glorious
adults.option_b: The ministry of death was shameful and the spirit is merely adequate
adults.option_c: Both ministries have equal glory
adults.option_d: The old ministry was more glorious than the new
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:7-8 — "The ministration of death, written and engraven in stones, was glorious... how shall not the ministration of the spirit be rather more glorious?"

adults.question: What does Paul say about the glory of the old covenant versus the new?
adults.option_a: That which was made glorious had no glory in this respect, by reason of the glory that excelleth
adults.option_b: The old covenant's glory continues unchanged
adults.option_c: Both covenants share identical glory
adults.option_d: The old covenant had no glory at all
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:10 — "For even that which was made glorious had no glory in this respect, by reason of the glory that excelleth."

adults.question: Why did Moses put a vail over his face?
adults.option_a: So the children of Israel could not stedfastly look to the end of that which is abolished
adults.option_b: To protect his face from the sun
adults.option_c: To hide his identity from the people
adults.option_d: To show humility before God
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:13 — "And not as Moses, which put a vail over his face, that the children of Israel could not stedfastly look to the end of that which is abolished."

adults.question: What happens when one turns to the Lord regarding the veil?
adults.option_a: The vail shall be taken away — now the Lord is that Spirit, and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty
adults.option_b: The veil becomes thicker
adults.option_c: The veil changes colour
adults.option_d: The veil is passed to someone else
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:16-17 — "Nevertheless when it shall turn to the Lord, the vail shall be taken away. Now the Lord is that Spirit: and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord"?
adults.option_a: With unveiled face we reflect the Lord's glory like a mirror and are progressively transformed into his image from glory to glory
adults.option_b: We see God perfectly and completely right now
adults.option_c: We see a distorted image that never changes
adults.option_d: We cannot see any of God's glory at all
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:18 — "But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord."

adults.question: What does Paul identify the "Lord" as in verse 17?
adults.option_a: Now the Lord is that Spirit — identifying the Holy Spirit's work in removing the veil and bringing freedom
adults.option_b: The Lord is only the Father
adults.option_c: The Lord refers to an angel
adults.option_d: The Lord is a metaphor for knowledge
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 3:17 — "Now the Lord is that Spirit: and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty."

kids.question: What did God command to shine out of darkness?
kids.option_a: Light
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:6 — "For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts."

kids.question: What kind of vessels (containers) does Paul say our bodies are like?
kids.option_a: Earthen vessels (clay pots)
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:7 — "But we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us."

kids.question: Can we see the things that last forever?
kids.option_a: No, eternal things are not seen; things we see are temporary
kids.option_b: Yes, everything we see lasts forever
kids.option_c: Only some things we see last forever
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:18 — "The things which are seen are temporal; but the things which are not seen are eternal."

teens.question: What does Paul say they have renounced?
teens.option_a: The hidden things of dishonesty — not walking in craftiness nor handling the word of God deceitfully
teens.option_b: The right to earn a living
teens.option_c: The hope of ever visiting Corinth
teens.option_d: The practice of speaking in tongues
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:2 — "But have renounced the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in craftiness, nor handling the word of God deceitfully."

teens.question: Who has blinded the minds of those who don't believe?
teens.option_a: The god of this world — lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ should shine unto them
teens.option_b: The Roman government
teens.option_c: The Jewish leaders
teens.option_d: The Greek philosophers
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:4 — "In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them."

teens.question: Why do we have treasure in earthen vessels?
teens.option_a: That the excellency of the power may be of God and not of us
teens.option_b: So people will admire us
teens.option_c: So we can show off our strength
teens.option_d: So the vessels become more valuable
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:7 — "But we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us."

teens.question: Though we are troubled, what does Paul say we are NOT?
teens.option_a: Distressed — troubled on every side yet not distressed; perplexed but not in despair
teens.option_b: Troubled at all
teens.option_c: Believers in Christ
teens.option_d: Qualified to preach
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:8 — "We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed; we are perplexed, but not in despair."

teens.question: What does Paul say about our light affliction?
teens.option_a: It worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory
teens.option_b: It has no purpose or meaning
teens.option_c: It is punishment for our sins
teens.option_d: It proves God has abandoned us
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:17 — "For our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory."

adults.question: How does Paul describe his ministry in light of God's mercy?
adults.option_a: As we have received mercy we faint not — commending himself by manifestation of the truth to every man's conscience in the sight of God
adults.option_b: As a heavy burden he reluctantly carries
adults.option_c: As a privilege reserved for the elite
adults.option_d: As a temporary assignment until retirement
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:1-2 — "Therefore seeing we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we faint not; But have renounced the hidden things of dishonesty... by manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God."

adults.question: If the gospel is hid, to whom is it hid?
adults.option_a: To them that are lost — in whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not
adults.option_b: To the uneducated and illiterate
adults.option_c: To the Gentile nations only
adults.option_d: To those who live far from Jerusalem
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:3-4 — "But if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost: In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not."

adults.question: Who does Paul say they preach?
adults.option_a: Not ourselves but Christ Jesus the Lord, and ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake
adults.option_b: Themselves as the primary authority
adults.option_c: A new philosophy of life
adults.option_d: The traditions of the Jewish elders
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:5 — "For we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord; and ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake."

adults.question: What has God shined in our hearts to give?
adults.option_a: The light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ
adults.option_b: The ability to perform miracles
adults.option_c: The wisdom of Solomon
adults.option_d: The power to judge others
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:6 — "For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts, to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ."

adults.question: What fourfold description of trials does Paul give in verses 8-9?
adults.option_a: Troubled but not distressed; perplexed but not in despair; persecuted but not forsaken; cast down but not destroyed
adults.option_b: Hungry, thirsty, cold, and homeless
adults.option_c: Beaten, imprisoned, shipwrecked, and stoned
adults.option_d: Mocked, rejected, betrayed, and abandoned
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:8-9 — "We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed; we are perplexed, but not in despair; Persecuted, but not forsaken; cast down, but not destroyed."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the dying of the Lord Jesus in their body?
adults.option_a: Always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body
adults.option_b: It is a burden that weakens their ministry
adults.option_c: It is a metaphor for emotional sadness
adults.option_d: It is a reference to baptism only
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:10 — "Always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body."

adults.question: What spirit of faith does Paul reference?
adults.option_a: We having the same spirit of faith according as it is written, I believed and therefore have I spoken — citing Psalm 116:10
adults.option_b: A spirit of timidity and fear
adults.option_c: A spirit of worldly wisdom
adults.option_d: A spirit of legalistic obedience
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:13 — "We having the same spirit of faith, according as it is written, I believed, and therefore have I spoken; we also believe, and therefore speak."

adults.question: What contrast does Paul draw between the outward and inward man?
adults.option_a: Though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day
adults.option_b: Both the outward and inward man perish together
adults.option_c: The outward man is strengthened while the inward weakens
adults.option_d: The inward man does not change at all
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 4:16 — "For which cause we faint not; but though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day."

kids.question: What does God have waiting for us in heaven?
kids.option_a: A house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:1 — "For we know that if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens."

kids.question: If anyone is in Christ, what are they?
kids.option_a: A new creature — old things are passed away and all things are become new
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:17 — "Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new."

kids.question: What job has God given believers?
kids.option_a: The ministry of reconciliation (bringing people back to God)
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:18 — "And hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation."

teens.question: What does Paul say about being absent from the body?
teens.option_a: To be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
teens.option_b: To be absent from the body is to sleep forever
teens.option_c: To be absent from the body is to cease to exist
teens.option_d: To be absent from the body is to wander in darkness
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:8 — "We are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body, and to be present with the Lord."

teens.question: Where must we all appear?
teens.option_a: Before the judgment seat of Christ, that every one may receive the things done in his body
teens.option_b: Before the Roman senate
teens.option_c: Before the Jewish Sanhedrin
teens.option_d: Before the angels in heaven
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:10 — "For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ; that every one may receive the things done in his body, according to that he hath done, whether it be good or bad."

teens.question: What constrains (compels) Paul?
teens.option_a: The love of Christ constraineth us — because if one died for all then were all dead
teens.option_b: The fear of punishment
teens.option_c: The desire for fame
teens.option_d: The need for money
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:14 — "For the love of Christ constraineth us; because we thus judge, that if one died for all, then were all dead."

teens.question: What does "if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature" mean?
teens.option_a: Old things are passed away and all things are become new — a complete spiritual transformation
teens.option_b: He becomes physically different
teens.option_c: He gets a new name
teens.option_d: He moves to a new city
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:17 — "Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new."

teens.question: What did God do with our sins through Christ?
teens.option_a: He made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
teens.option_b: He ignored our sins entirely
teens.option_c: He gave us a list of rules to follow
teens.option_d: He reduced the punishment by half
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:21 — "For he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the earthly house of this tabernacle?
adults.option_a: If it were dissolved, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens
adults.option_b: It will be renovated and made eternal
adults.option_c: It will be transferred to another person
adults.option_d: It has no relationship to the heavenly body
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:1 — "For we know that if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens."

adults.question: What has God wrought (prepared) us for and what has he given as the earnest?
adults.option_a: God hath wrought us for this selfsame thing and given unto us the earnest of the Spirit
adults.option_b: God has prepared wealth and given us gold
adults.option_c: God has wrought punishment and given a warning
adults.option_d: God has wrought confusion and given us riddles
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:5 — "Now he that hath wrought us for the selfsame thing is God, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the Spirit."

adults.question: What does Paul say about walking by faith?
adults.option_a: We walk by faith, not by sight — being always confident knowing that whilst in the body we are absent from the Lord
adults.option_b: We walk by sight and not by faith
adults.option_c: Faith and sight are identical things
adults.option_d: Walking by faith means ignoring all evidence
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:6-7 — "Therefore we are always confident, knowing that, whilst we are at home in the body, we are absent from the Lord: (For we walk by faith, not by sight.)"

adults.question: What does Paul say about knowing Christ after the flesh?
adults.option_a: Though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now henceforth know we him no more — we now know him spiritually
adults.option_b: We should focus only on Christ's earthly life
adults.option_c: Physical knowledge of Christ is most important
adults.option_d: We never knew Christ in any way
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:16 — "Wherefore henceforth know we no man after the flesh: yea, though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now henceforth know we him no more."

adults.question: What is the ministry of reconciliation that God has given?
adults.option_a: That God was in Christ reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them, and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation
adults.option_b: A ministry of judgment and condemnation
adults.option_c: A ministry limited to the Jewish people
adults.option_d: A ministry of philosophical debate
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:19 — "To wit, that God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them; and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation."

adults.question: What role does Paul say believers hold?
adults.option_a: Ambassadors for Christ — as though God did beseech you by us, we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God
adults.option_b: Judges over the nations
adults.option_c: Kings of earthly kingdoms
adults.option_d: Servants of the Roman government
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:20 — "Now then we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God did beseech you by us: we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of verse 21?
adults.option_a: The great exchange: Christ, who knew no sin, was made sin for us that we might be made the righteousness of God in him — imputed righteousness
adults.option_b: Christ merely demonstrated how to live righteously
adults.option_c: God punished Christ for his own sins
adults.option_d: Righteousness is earned through good works
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:21 — "For he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him."

adults.question: What does Paul labour for, whether present or absent?
adults.option_a: That we may be accepted of him — we labour to be well-pleasing to the Lord
adults.option_b: To build the largest church possible
adults.option_c: To earn the highest heavenly reward
adults.option_d: To impress the other apostles
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 5:9 — "Wherefore we labour, that, whether present or absent, we may be accepted of him."

kids.question: When is the right time to be saved?
kids.option_a: Now — today is the day of salvation
kids.option_b: Only when you are very old
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:2 — "Behold, now is the accepted time; behold, now is the day of salvation."

kids.question: What does God promise to be to us?
kids.option_a: A Father — and we shall be his sons and daughters
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:18 — "And will be a Father unto you, and ye shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty."

kids.question: What should believers do with people who don't believe?
kids.option_a: Not be unequally yoked (joined) together with unbelievers
kids.option_b: Avoid them completely at all times
kids.option_c: Do whatever they want us to do
kids.option_d: Pretend to agree with everything they say
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:14 — "Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers."

teens.question: What does Paul urge the Corinthians not to receive in vain?
teens.option_a: The grace of God — for now is the accepted time, now is the day of salvation
teens.option_b: Their financial gifts to the church
teens.option_c: The letters Paul sent to them
teens.option_d: The food provided at church meals
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:1-2 — "We then, as workers together with him, beseech you also that ye receive not the grace of God in vain... behold, now is the accepted time; behold, now is the day of salvation."

teens.question: How does Paul describe his ministry to give no offence?
teens.option_a: In all things approving ourselves as ministers of God — in much patience, afflictions, necessities, distresses
teens.option_b: By avoiding all difficult situations
teens.option_c: By always agreeing with everyone
teens.option_d: By staying silent about controversial truths
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:4 — "But in all things approving ourselves as the ministers of God, in much patience, in afflictions, in necessities, in distresses."

teens.question: What rhetorical questions does Paul ask about believers and unbelievers?
teens.option_a: What fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? What communion hath light with darkness?
teens.option_b: How much money should they share?
teens.option_c: What language should they speak together?
teens.option_d: Where should they live in the same city?
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:14 — "For what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? and what communion hath light with darkness?"

teens.question: What does Paul say believers are the temple of?
teens.option_a: The living God — as God hath said, I will dwell in them and walk in them
teens.option_b: The temple of human achievement
teens.option_c: The temple of Roman culture
teens.option_d: The temple of earthly wisdom
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:16 — "For ye are the temple of the living God; as God hath said, I will dwell in them, and walk in them; and I will be their God, and they shall be my people."

teens.question: What does God tell his people to do in verse 17?
teens.option_a: Come out from among them, be separate, and touch not the unclean thing — and God will receive them
teens.option_b: Join every group they can find
teens.option_c: Blend in with the surrounding culture
teens.option_d: Fight against the Roman government
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:17 — "Wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing; and I will receive you."

adults.question: What Old Testament passage does Paul quote in verse 2?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 49:8 — "In an acceptable time have I heard thee, and in a day of salvation have I succoured thee"
adults.option_b: Psalm 23:1
adults.option_c: Genesis 1:1
adults.option_d: Deuteronomy 6:4
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:2 — "For he saith, I have heard thee in a time accepted, and in the day of salvation have I succoured thee." This quotes Isaiah 49:8.

adults.question: What extensive catalogue of hardships does Paul list in verses 4-5?
adults.option_a: Patience, afflictions, necessities, distresses, stripes, imprisonments, tumults, labours, watchings, fastings
adults.option_b: Wealth, power, fame, comfort, and luxury
adults.option_c: Education, travel, study, and debate
adults.option_d: Health, rest, peace, and quiet
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:4-5 — "In much patience, in afflictions, in necessities, in distresses, In stripes, in imprisonments, in tumults, in labours, in watchings, in fastings."

adults.question: What paradoxes does Paul describe about the apostolic ministry in verses 8-10?
adults.option_a: As unknown yet well known; dying yet living; chastened yet not killed; sorrowful yet always rejoicing; poor yet making many rich; having nothing yet possessing all things
adults.option_b: As wealthy yet poor; famous yet unknown
adults.option_c: As powerful yet weak; honoured yet dishonoured
adults.option_d: As young yet old; travelling yet stationary
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:8-10 — "By honour and dishonour, by evil report and good report: as deceivers, and yet true; As unknown, and yet well known; as dying, and yet, behold, we live; as chastened, and not killed; As sorrowful, yet alway rejoicing; as poor, yet making many rich; as having nothing, and yet possessing all things."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers"?
adults.option_a: Believers should not enter binding partnerships with unbelievers that compromise their faith — the metaphor is from Deuteronomy 22:10 about yoking different animals together
adults.option_b: Christians should never interact with non-Christians
adults.option_c: Believers should not do business of any kind
adults.option_d: Only marriages between believers and unbelievers are forbidden
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:14 — "Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers: for what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness?" The imagery comes from Deuteronomy 22:10.

adults.question: What five rhetorical questions does Paul ask in verses 14-16?
adults.option_a: What fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? What communion hath light with darkness? What concord hath Christ with Belial? What part hath he that believeth with an infidel? What agreement hath the temple of God with idols?
adults.option_b: Five questions about church finances
adults.option_c: Five questions about eschatology
adults.option_d: Five questions about spiritual gifts
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:14-16 lists these five contrasting pairs to emphasize the incompatibility of believers being bound with unbelievers.

adults.question: What does Paul say about the heart of the apostles toward the Corinthians?
adults.option_a: Our mouth is open unto you, our heart is enlarged — ye are not straitened in us but ye are straitened in your own bowels
adults.option_b: He has closed his heart to them
adults.option_c: He has grown indifferent toward them
adults.option_d: He has transferred his affection to another church
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:11-12 — "O ye Corinthians, our mouth is open unto you, our heart is enlarged. Ye are not straitened in us, but ye are straitened in your own bowels."

adults.question: What recompence (return) does Paul ask from the Corinthians?
adults.option_a: Be ye also enlarged — open your hearts to us as we have opened ours to you
adults.option_b: Send a large financial gift
adults.option_c: Build a monument in his honour
adults.option_d: Send workers to help his ministry
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:13 — "Now for a recompence in the same, (I speak as unto my children,) be ye also enlarged."

adults.question: What comprehensive promise does God make in verses 16-18?
adults.option_a: I will dwell in them, walk in them, be their God; they shall be my people; I will receive you; I will be a Father unto you, ye shall be my sons and daughters
adults.option_b: He will make them wealthy and powerful
adults.option_c: He will give them political authority
adults.option_d: He will remove all suffering from their lives
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:16-18 — God promises indwelling presence, covenant relationship, reception, and familial belonging, drawing from Leviticus 26:12, Isaiah 52:11, and 2 Samuel 7:14.

kids.question: What kind of sorrow makes us come back to God?
kids.option_a: Godly sorrow (being sorry because we disobeyed God)
kids.option_d: Being sad about losing a toy
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:10 — "For godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of."

kids.question: Who brought good news to Paul about the Corinthians?
kids.option_a: Titus
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:6-7 — "Nevertheless God, that comforteth those that are cast down, comforted us by the coming of Titus."

kids.question: Was Paul glad he had written a strong letter to the Corinthians?
kids.option_a: Yes, because it helped them repent and turn back to God
kids.option_b: No, he wished he had never written it
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:9 — "Now I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry, but that ye sorrowed to repentance."

teens.question: What does Paul urge them to do since they have God's promises?
teens.option_a: Cleanse themselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God
teens.option_b: Celebrate with feasts and parties
teens.option_c: Withdraw from all social activities
teens.option_d: Focus entirely on earning money
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:1 — "Having therefore these promises, dearly beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God."

teens.question: How did God comfort Paul when he was cast down?
teens.option_a: By the coming of Titus, and by the consolation wherewith Titus was comforted in them
teens.option_b: By sending an angel with a message
teens.option_c: By giving him a vision of heaven
teens.option_d: By removing all his enemies
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:6-7 — "Nevertheless God, that comforteth those that are cast down, comforted us by the coming of Titus; And not by his coming only, but by the consolation wherewith he was comforted in you."

teens.question: What is the difference between godly sorrow and worldly sorrow?
teens.option_a: Godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of; but the sorrow of the world worketh death
teens.option_b: There is no difference between them
teens.option_c: Worldly sorrow is more intense than godly sorrow
teens.option_d: Godly sorrow is weaker than worldly sorrow
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:10 — "For godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of: but the sorrow of the world worketh death."

teens.question: What did the Corinthians' godly sorrow produce in them?
teens.option_a: Carefulness, clearing of themselves, indignation, fear, vehement desire, zeal, and revenge (vindication)
teens.option_b: Laziness and indifference
teens.option_c: Pride and self-righteousness
teens.option_d: Depression and hopelessness
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:11 — "For behold this selfsame thing, that ye sorrowed after a godly sort, what carefulness it wrought in you, yea, what clearing of yourselves, yea, what indignation, yea, what fear, yea, what vehement desire, yea, what zeal, yea, what revenge!"

teens.question: How did Titus feel about the Corinthians?
teens.option_a: His inward affection was more abundant toward them, remembering their obedience and how they received him with fear and trembling
teens.option_b: He was angry and disappointed
teens.option_c: He was afraid to return to them
teens.option_d: He had no particular feelings about them
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:15 — "And his inward affection is more abundant toward you, whilst he remembereth the obedience of you all, how with fear and trembling ye received him."

adults.question: What twofold cleansing does Paul call for in verse 1?
adults.option_a: Cleansing from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit — both outward behaviour and inward attitudes
adults.option_b: Cleansing only from spiritual sins
adults.option_c: Cleansing only from physical sins
adults.option_d: Cleansing through ritual washing only
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:1 — "Let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God."

adults.question: What does Paul say about his boasting regarding the Corinthians to Titus?
adults.option_a: He was not ashamed — his boasting to Titus proved true, just as all things he spoke were truth
adults.option_b: He regretted exaggerating about them
adults.option_c: He never mentioned them to Titus
adults.option_d: He criticised them before Titus
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:14 — "For if I have boasted any thing to him of you, I am not ashamed; but as we spake all things to you in truth, even so our boasting, which I made before Titus, is found a truth."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the sorrow his letter caused?
adults.option_a: He did repent at first, but now rejoices — not that they were made sorry but that they sorrowed to repentance after a godly manner
adults.option_b: He deeply regrets ever sending the letter
adults.option_c: He wished the letter had been harsher
adults.option_d: He plans to send an even more severe letter
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:8-9 — "For though I made you sorry with a letter, I do not repent, though I did repent: for I perceive that the same epistle hath made you sorry, though it were but for a season. Now I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry, but that ye sorrowed to repentance."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the Corinthians' sorrow being after a godly manner?
adults.option_a: They received damage by Paul in nothing — godly sorrow caused no spiritual harm but led to genuine repentance
adults.option_b: It caused them to leave the church
adults.option_c: It made them bitter toward Paul
adults.option_d: It led them to reject the gospel
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:9 — "Now I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry, but that ye sorrowed to repentance: for ye were made sorry after a godly manner, that ye might receive damage by us in nothing."

adults.question: What seven fruits of godly repentance does Paul list in verse 11?
adults.option_a: Carefulness, clearing of themselves, indignation, fear, vehement desire, zeal, and revenge (vindication of justice)
adults.option_b: Faith, hope, love, joy, peace, patience, and kindness
adults.option_c: Wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, fear, and delight
adults.option_d: Poverty, chastity, obedience, prayer, fasting, alms, and pilgrimage
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:11 — "What carefulness it wrought in you, yea, what clearing of yourselves, yea, what indignation, yea, what fear, yea, what vehement desire, yea, what zeal, yea, what revenge!"

adults.question: Why did Paul write the severe letter?
adults.option_a: Not for his cause that had done the wrong, nor for his cause that suffered wrong, but that their care for Paul might appear unto them in the sight of God
adults.option_b: Primarily to punish the offender
adults.option_c: To assert his apostolic authority
adults.option_d: To demand financial compensation
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:12 — "Wherefore, though I wrote unto you, I did it not for his cause that had done the wrong, nor for his cause that suffered wrong, but that our care for you in the sight of God might appear unto you."

adults.question: What was Paul's emotional state when he arrived in Macedonia?
adults.option_a: His flesh had no rest — without were fightings, within were fears; but God comforted him by the coming of Titus
adults.option_b: He was joyful and at complete peace
adults.option_c: He was angry at the Corinthians
adults.option_d: He was indifferent about everything
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:5-6 — "For, when we were come into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest, but we were troubled on every side; without were fightings, within were fears. Nevertheless God, that comforteth those that are cast down, comforted us by the coming of Titus."

adults.question: What does Paul conclude about his confidence in the Corinthians?
adults.option_a: He has confidence in them in all things — he rejoiceth the more exceedingly
adults.option_b: He has lost all trust in them
adults.option_c: He is cautiously optimistic but uncertain
adults.option_d: He will withhold judgment until his next visit
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 7:16 — "I rejoice therefore that I have confidence in you in all things."

kids.question: What did the churches in Macedonia do even though they were poor?
kids.option_a: They gave generously to help other Christians
kids.option_b: They kept all their money for themselves
kids.option_c: They asked Paul for more money
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:2 — "How that in a great trial of affliction the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their liberality."

kids.question: What did Jesus do for us even though he was rich?
kids.option_a: He became poor so that we could become rich
kids.option_b: He stayed rich and gave us gold
kids.option_c: He kept his riches in heaven
kids.option_d: He asked us to make him richer
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:9 — "For ye know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that, though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich."

kids.question: Who did Paul send to help the Corinthians with their giving?
kids.option_a: Titus
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:6 — "Insomuch that we desired Titus, that as he had begun, so he would also finish in you the same grace also."

teens.question: How did the Macedonian churches give beyond Paul's expectation?
teens.option_a: They first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto Paul by the will of God — and gave beyond their power willingly
teens.option_b: They gave exactly what was requested
teens.option_c: They gave only after much persuasion
teens.option_d: They gave reluctantly and with complaints
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:3-5 — "For to their power, I bear record, yea, and beyond their power they were willing of themselves... and this they did, not as we hoped, but first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us by the will of God."

teens.question: What seven things does Paul say the Corinthians abound in?
teens.option_a: Faith, utterance, knowledge, all diligence, love — Paul urges them to abound in this grace of giving also
teens.option_b: Gold, silver, land, houses, and servants
teens.option_c: Miracles, signs, wonders, and healings
teens.option_d: Political power, social status, and fame
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:7 — "Therefore, as ye abound in every thing, in faith, and utterance, and knowledge, and in all diligence, and in your love to us, see that ye abound in this grace also."

teens.question: What principle does Paul give about willing giving?
teens.option_a: If there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not
teens.option_b: Give only if you can afford it after all your needs
teens.option_c: Give everything you have with nothing left
teens.option_d: Give only what is specifically demanded
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:12 — "For if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not."

teens.question: What does Paul say about equality in giving?
teens.option_a: That there be equality — your abundance now should supply their want, and their abundance may supply your want
teens.option_b: The rich should give everything to the poor
teens.option_c: Everyone should give exactly the same amount
teens.option_d: Only the wealthiest members should give
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:14 — "But by an equality, that now at this time your abundance may be a supply for their want, that their abundance also may be a supply for your want: that there may be equality."

teens.question: What Old Testament example does Paul cite about equality?
teens.option_a: He that had gathered much had nothing over, and he that had gathered little had no lack — referring to the manna
teens.option_b: The division of the promised land
teens.option_c: The building of Solomon's temple
teens.option_d: The offering of Abraham's tithe
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:15 — "As it is written, He that had gathered much had nothing over; and he that had gathered little had no lack." This quotes Exodus 16:18 about gathering manna.

adults.question: What does Paul reveal about the grace of God bestowed on the Macedonian churches?
adults.option_a: In a great trial of affliction, the abundance of their joy and deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their liberality
adults.option_b: God gave them great financial prosperity
adults.option_c: They received miraculous provision of food
adults.option_d: They were exempted from the Roman tax
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:1-2 — "Moreover, brethren, we do you to wit of the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia; How that in a great trial of affliction the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their liberality."

adults.question: What is the Christological basis for generous giving in verse 9?
adults.option_a: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ: though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich
adults.option_b: The example of Abraham tithing to Melchizedek
adults.option_c: The command of Moses regarding temple offerings
adults.option_d: The parable of the talents
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:9 — "For ye know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that, though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the Corinthians' readiness a year ago?
adults.option_a: They were the first to begin, not only to do but also to be forward (willing) a year ago — now he urges them to complete what they started
adults.option_b: They had already completed the collection
adults.option_c: They had never shown any interest in giving
adults.option_d: They had given more than any other church
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:10-11 — "For this is expedient for you, who have begun before, not only to do, but also to be forward a year ago. Now therefore perform the doing of it."

adults.question: How does Paul describe Titus' role in the collection?
adults.option_a: Titus accepted the exhortation and being more forward went of his own accord unto them — he is Paul's partner and fellowhelper
adults.option_b: Titus reluctantly agreed to go
adults.option_c: Titus refused the assignment
adults.option_d: Titus went only because Paul commanded him
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:17,23 — "For indeed he accepted the exhortation; but being more forward, of his own accord he went unto you... he is my partner and fellowhelper concerning you."

adults.question: Who is the brother "whose praise is in the gospel throughout all the churches"?
adults.option_a: An unnamed brother chosen by the churches to travel with Paul, whose praise in the gospel is widely known
adults.option_b: Luke, the physician and author
adults.option_c: Barnabas, Paul's former companion
adults.option_d: Apollos, the eloquent teacher
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:18-19 — "And we have sent with him the brother, whose praise is in the gospel throughout all the churches; And not that only, but who was also chosen of the churches to travel with us."

adults.question: Why does Paul take precautions about the administration of the gift?
adults.option_a: Avoiding that any man should blame them in this abundance — providing for honest things not only before the Lord but also before men
adults.option_b: To ensure the gift reaches the maximum amount
adults.option_c: To track each individual's donation
adults.option_d: To create a public record for tax purposes
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:20-21 — "Avoiding this, that no man should blame us in this abundance which is administered by us: Providing for honest things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men."

adults.question: What does Paul call the messengers of the churches?
adults.option_a: The messengers of the churches and the glory of Christ
adults.option_b: The servants of the apostles
adults.option_c: The rulers of the congregations
adults.option_d: The prophets of the new covenant
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:23 — "Whether any do enquire of Titus, he is my partner and fellowhelper concerning you: or our brethren be enquired of, they are the messengers of the churches, and the glory of Christ."

adults.question: What proof does Paul ask the Corinthians to show?
adults.option_a: The proof of their love and of Paul's boasting on their behalf before the churches
adults.option_b: The proof of their theological knowledge
adults.option_c: The proof of their spiritual gifts
adults.option_d: The proof of their church attendance
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 8:24 — "Wherefore shew ye to them, and before the churches, the proof of your love, and of our boasting on your behalf."

kids.question: What kind of giver does God love?
kids.option_a: A cheerful giver
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:7 — "God loveth a cheerful giver."

kids.question: If you plant a lot of seeds, what happens?
kids.option_a: You get a big harvest — he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:6 — "He which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully."

kids.question: What special gift from God can we never fully describe?
kids.option_a: His unspeakable gift (Jesus)
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:15 — "Thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift."

teens.question: Why did Paul send the brethren ahead to Corinth?
teens.option_a: That their bounty might be ready as a matter of bounty and not as of covetousness
teens.option_b: To start new churches in the area
teens.option_c: To collect payment for Paul's expenses
teens.option_d: To investigate reports of false teaching
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:5 — "Therefore I thought it necessary to exhort the brethren, that they would go before unto you, and make up beforehand your bounty... that the same might be ready, as a matter of bounty, and not as of covetousness."

teens.question: How should each person decide what to give?
teens.option_a: Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, not grudgingly or of necessity, for God loveth a cheerful giver
teens.option_b: According to a fixed percentage required by the church
teens.option_c: Based on what others are giving
teens.option_d: Only what the apostles demand
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:7 — "Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver."

teens.question: What does Paul say God is able to make abound toward believers?
teens.option_a: All grace — that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work
teens.option_b: Only financial blessings
teens.option_c: Only spiritual gifts
teens.option_d: Only physical health
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:8 — "And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work."

teens.question: What does the Corinthians' generosity cause others to do?
teens.option_a: Glorify God — their generosity causes thanksgiving to God and proves their obedience to the gospel
teens.option_b: Become jealous of the Corinthians
teens.option_c: Leave their own churches
teens.option_d: Start competing with each other
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:13 — "Whiles by the experiment of this ministration they glorify God for your professed subjection unto the gospel of Christ, and for your liberal distribution unto them, and unto all men."

teens.question: What is God's "unspeakable gift"?
teens.option_a: The gift of Jesus Christ — a gift so wonderful it cannot be fully expressed in words
teens.option_b: A treasure chest of gold
teens.option_c: The ability to speak in tongues
teens.option_d: A special prayer language
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:15 — "Thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift." This refers to the supreme gift of God's Son.

adults.question: What does Paul say about the Corinthians' readiness to give?
adults.option_a: He boasted to Macedonia that Achaia (Corinth) was ready a year ago, and their zeal provoked very many
adults.option_b: The Corinthians had already completed the collection
adults.option_c: The Corinthians showed no interest in giving
adults.option_d: The Corinthians were the least generous church
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:2 — "For I know the forwardness of your mind, for which I boast of you to them of Macedonia, that Achaia was ready a year ago; and your zeal hath provoked very many."

adults.question: What agricultural principle does Paul apply to giving?
adults.option_a: He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully
adults.option_b: Those who sow will always reap exactly the same amount
adults.option_c: Sowing and reaping have no connection
adults.option_d: Only professional farmers can reap a harvest
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:6 — "But this I say, He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully."

adults.question: What Old Testament passage does Paul reference about God's provision?
adults.option_a: He hath dispersed abroad; he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever — quoting Psalm 112:9
adults.option_b: The provision of manna in the wilderness
adults.option_c: The feeding of Elijah by ravens
adults.option_d: The multiplication of the widow's oil
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:9 — "As it is written, He hath dispersed abroad; he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever." This quotes Psalm 112:9.

adults.question: What does Paul say about God supplying seed to the sower?
adults.option_a: He that ministereth seed to the sower both minister bread for your food, and multiply your seed sown, and increase the fruits of your righteousness
adults.option_b: God gives seed only to wealthy farmers
adults.option_c: Seed is always in limited supply
adults.option_d: Sowers must provide their own seed
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:10 — "Now he that ministereth seed to the sower both minister bread for your food, and multiply your seed sown, and increase the fruits of your righteousness."

adults.question: What is the purpose of being enriched in every thing?
adults.option_a: Being enriched in every thing to all bountifulness, which causeth through us thanksgiving to God
adults.option_b: To accumulate personal wealth
adults.option_c: To gain social status in the church
adults.option_d: To prove one's spiritual maturity
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:11 — "Being enriched in every thing to all bountifulness, which causeth through us thanksgiving to God."

adults.question: What does Paul say the administration of this service does?
adults.option_a: It not only supplieth the want of the saints but is abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God
adults.option_b: It creates division in the church
adults.option_c: It burdens the wealthy members
adults.option_d: It causes jealousy among the recipients
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:12 — "For the administration of this service not only supplieth the want of the saints, but is abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God."

adults.question: What do the recipients pray for regarding the Corinthians?
adults.option_a: They long after the Corinthians and pray for them because of the exceeding grace of God in them
adults.option_b: They demand more financial support
adults.option_c: They criticise the Corinthians' doctrine
adults.option_d: They request the Corinthians visit them
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:14 — "And by their prayer for you, which long after you for the exceeding grace of God in you."

adults.question: How does Paul describe the "all sufficiency" God provides?
adults.option_a: Always having all sufficiency in all things — the Greek word "autarkeia" means contentment and self-sufficiency that comes from God's provision enabling every good work
adults.option_b: A bare minimum to survive
adults.option_c: Unlimited material wealth
adults.option_d: Only spiritual blessings with no material provision
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:8 — "And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work."

kids.question: What kind of weapons does Paul say Christians use?
kids.option_a: Not swords or spears — our weapons are mighty through God
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:4 — "For the weapons of our warfare are not carnal, but mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds."

kids.question: What should we bring every thought to obey?
kids.option_a: Christ — bringing every thought into captivity to the obedience of Christ
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:5 — "Bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ."

kids.question: Who is the right person to praise you?
kids.option_a: The Lord — not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:18 — "For not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth."

teens.question: What are the weapons of our warfare?
teens.option_a: Not carnal but mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds
teens.option_b: Made of the finest Roman steel
teens.option_c: Only the sword of the Spirit
teens.option_d: Human arguments and debate skills
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:4 — "For the weapons of our warfare are not carnal, but mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds."

teens.question: What do we cast down with these spiritual weapons?
teens.option_a: Imaginations, and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God
teens.option_b: Physical fortresses and city walls
teens.option_c: Enemy armies and their equipment
teens.option_d: Only personal sins and habits
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:5 — "Casting down imaginations, and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ."

teens.question: What does Paul say about comparing themselves with others?
teens.option_a: They that measure themselves by themselves and compare themselves among themselves are not wise
teens.option_b: Comparison is helpful for growth
teens.option_c: Christians should always compete with each other
teens.option_d: Self-comparison is the best motivation
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:12 — "For we dare not make ourselves of the number, or compare ourselves with some that commend themselves: but they measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves among themselves, are not wise."

teens.question: What does Paul say about boasting?
teens.option_a: He that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord — quoting Jeremiah 9:24
teens.option_b: Boasting about achievements is always good
teens.option_c: Boasting builds confidence in others
teens.option_d: Boasting is acceptable for church leaders
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:17 — "But he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord."

teens.question: What does Paul say about his authority from the Lord?
teens.option_a: The Lord gave him authority for edification (building up) and not for destruction of the Corinthians
teens.option_b: His authority was unlimited with no boundaries
teens.option_c: His authority was only over the Corinthian church
teens.option_d: His authority was given by the Jerusalem council
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:8 — "For though I should boast somewhat more of our authority, which the Lord hath given us for edification, and not for your destruction, I should not be ashamed."

adults.question: What accusation did Paul's opponents make about his presence?
adults.option_a: His bodily presence is weak and his speech contemptible — but Paul warns that what he is in word by letters when absent, such will he be in deed when present
adults.option_b: He was too aggressive and intimidating
adults.option_c: He was too wealthy and arrogant
adults.option_d: He was too young and inexperienced
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:10-11 — "For his letters, say they, are weighty and powerful; but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible. Let such an one think this, that, such as we are in word by letters when we are absent, such will we be also in deed when we are present."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the measure or rule God has distributed to him?
adults.option_a: God has distributed to him a measure to reach even unto the Corinthians — he is not overextending himself because he came to them with the gospel of Christ
adults.option_b: His measure was limited to Jerusalem only
adults.option_c: His measure covered only the Jewish diaspora
adults.option_d: His measure excluded the Greek-speaking world
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:13-14 — "But we will not boast of things without our measure, but according to the measure of the rule which God hath distributed to us, a measure to reach even unto you. For we stretch not ourselves beyond our measure, as though we reached not unto you: for we are come as far as to you also in preaching the gospel of Christ."

adults.question: What does Paul hope will happen as the Corinthians' faith increases?
adults.option_a: He will be enlarged by them according to his rule abundantly, to preach the gospel in regions beyond them
adults.option_b: They will send him more financial support
adults.option_c: They will build a larger church building
adults.option_d: They will stop all contact with other apostles
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:15-16 — "Not boasting of things without our measure, that is, of other men's labours; but having hope, when your faith is increased, that we shall be enlarged by you according to our rule abundantly, To preach the gospel in the regions beyond you."

adults.question: What does Paul say about those who commend themselves?
adults.option_a: Not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth
adults.option_b: Self-commendation is always appropriate for leaders
adults.option_c: Those who commend themselves deserve honour
adults.option_d: Self-promotion is necessary for effective ministry
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:18 — "For not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth."

adults.question: What does "bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ" mean?
adults.option_a: Every intellectual and philosophical argument must be submitted to Christ's lordship — the mind is a battlefield where obedience to Christ must be established
adults.option_b: Only sinful thoughts need to be controlled
adults.option_c: Thinking is discouraged for believers
adults.option_d: Only theological thoughts matter to Christ
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:5 — "Casting down imaginations, and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ."

adults.question: What does Paul say about not boasting in another man's line of things?
adults.option_a: He will not boast of things without his measure or of other men's labours — he builds on his own apostolic foundation, not another's
adults.option_b: He freely takes credit for others' work
adults.option_c: He considers all churches equally his
adults.option_d: He competes with other apostles for credit
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:15-16 — "Not boasting of things without our measure, that is, of other men's labours; but having hope... To preach the gospel in the regions beyond you, and not to boast in another man's line of things made ready to our hand."

adults.question: By what meekness and gentleness does Paul beseech the Corinthians?
adults.option_a: By the meekness and gentleness of Christ — though some think Paul is base (bold only in letters, timid in person)
adults.option_b: By the authority of the Jerusalem council
adults.option_c: By the power of the Roman government
adults.option_d: By the traditions of the Pharisees
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:1 — "Now I Paul myself beseech you by the meekness and gentleness of Christ, who in presence am base among you, but being absent am bold toward you."

adults.question: What readiness does Paul have regarding disobedience?
adults.option_a: A readiness to revenge all disobedience when the Corinthians' obedience is fulfilled — disciplinary action is postponed to give them time to comply
adults.option_b: Immediate punishment without warning
adults.option_c: Complete indifference to disobedience
adults.option_d: A plan to report them to Roman authorities
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 10:6 — "And having in a readiness to revenge all disobedience, when your obedience is fulfilled."

kids.question: Who can disguise himself as an angel of light?
kids.option_a: Satan
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:14 — "And no marvel; for Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light."

kids.question: How many times was Paul shipwrecked?
kids.option_a: Three times
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:25 — "Thrice I suffered shipwreck."

kids.question: Did Paul take money from the Corinthians for preaching?
kids.option_a: No, he preached the gospel to them freely
kids.option_b: Yes, he charged a high price
kids.option_c: Yes, but only a small amount
kids.option_d: He asked for gold and silver
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:7 — "Have I committed an offence in abasing myself that ye might be exalted, because I have preached to you the gospel of God freely?"

teens.question: What does Paul fear about the Corinthians being deceived?
teens.option_a: As the serpent beguiled Eve through his subtilty, their minds should be corrupted from the simplicity that is in Christ
teens.option_b: That they will forget how to read Scripture
teens.option_c: That they will stop attending church
teens.option_d: That they will move to another city
