teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:3 — "But I fear, lest by any means, as the serpent beguiled Eve through his subtilty, so your minds should be corrupted from the simplicity that is in Christ."

teens.question: What does Paul call the false teachers at Corinth?
teens.option_a: False apostles, deceitful workers, transforming themselves into the apostles of Christ
teens.option_b: Honourable leaders with different opinions
teens.option_c: Well-meaning but misguided teachers
teens.option_d: Temporary substitutes for Paul
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:13 — "For such are false apostles, deceitful workers, transforming themselves into the apostles of Christ."

teens.question: How does Satan disguise himself?
teens.option_a: Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light — therefore his ministers also transform themselves as ministers of righteousness
teens.option_b: He appears as a terrifying monster
teens.option_c: He never disguises himself
teens.option_d: He only appears as a serpent
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:14-15 — "And no marvel; for Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light. Therefore it is no great thing if his ministers also be transformed as the ministers of righteousness."

teens.question: What kinds of dangers did Paul face in his travels?
teens.option_a: Perils of waters, robbers, his own countrymen, the heathen, the city, the wilderness, the sea, and false brethren
teens.option_b: Only dangers from wild animals
teens.option_c: Only dangers from the Roman government
teens.option_d: He faced no real dangers at all
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:26 — "In journeyings often, in perils of waters, in perils of robbers, in perils by mine own countrymen, in perils by the heathen, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness, in perils in the sea, in perils among false brethren."

teens.question: What daily burden did Paul carry besides physical suffering?
teens.option_a: The care of all the churches — who is weak and Paul is not weak? Who is offended and he burns not?
teens.option_b: The weight of his personal finances
teens.option_c: The responsibility of running a business
teens.option_d: The task of writing Roman law
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:28-29 — "Beside those things that are without, that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches. Who is weak, and I am not weak? who is offended, and I burn not?"

adults.question: What does Paul say about presenting the Corinthians to Christ?
adults.option_a: He espoused them to one husband, to present them as a chaste virgin to Christ — jealous with a godly jealousy
adults.option_b: He wanted them to be independent of Christ
adults.option_c: He planned for them to follow multiple teachers
adults.option_d: He had no particular concern for their faithfulness
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:2 — "For I am jealous over you with godly jealousy: for I have espoused you to one husband, that I may present you as a chaste virgin to Christ."

adults.question: What does Paul accuse the false teachers of preaching?
adults.option_a: Another Jesus whom Paul had not preached, another spirit, and another gospel
adults.option_b: The same gospel with minor variations
adults.option_c: A complementary message to Paul's teaching
adults.option_d: A more advanced version of Paul's gospel
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:4 — "For if he that cometh preacheth another Jesus, whom we have not preached, or if ye receive another spirit, which ye have not received, or another gospel, which ye have not accepted, ye might well bear with him."

adults.question: What does Paul say about being inferior to the "chiefest apostles"?
adults.option_a: He is not a whit behind the very chiefest apostles — though he be rude in speech, yet not in knowledge
adults.option_b: He admits he is inferior to them
adults.option_c: He considers them his superiors in every way
adults.option_d: He has never met the chiefest apostles
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:5-6 — "For I suppose I was not a whit behind the very chiefest apostles. But though I be rude in speech, yet not in knowledge."

adults.question: How did Paul fund his ministry at Corinth?
adults.option_a: He robbed other churches, taking wages of them to serve the Corinthians, and when in want the brethren from Macedonia supplied him
adults.option_b: The Corinthians paid him a generous salary
adults.option_c: He worked as a tentmaker only
adults.option_d: He received funding from the Roman government
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:8-9 — "I robbed other churches, taking wages of them, to do you service. And when I was present with you, and wanted, I was chargeable to no man: for that which was lacking to me the brethren which came from Macedonia supplied."

adults.question: What physical sufferings does Paul catalogue in verses 24-25?
adults.option_a: Five times forty stripes save one from the Jews; thrice beaten with rods; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; a night and a day in the deep
adults.option_b: He was never physically harmed
adults.option_c: He was imprisoned once and beaten twice
adults.option_d: He suffered only verbal persecution
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:24-25 — "Of the Jews five times received I forty stripes save one. Thrice was I beaten with rods, once was I stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have been in the deep."

adults.question: What additional hardships does Paul list in verse 27?
adults.option_a: Weariness and painfulness, watchings often, hunger and thirst, fastings often, cold and nakedness
adults.option_b: Wealth, comfort, and luxurious living
adults.option_c: Only spiritual struggles and prayer
adults.option_d: Academic challenges and debate
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:27 — "In weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness."

adults.question: If Paul must boast, what will he boast of?
adults.option_a: The things which concern his infirmities (weaknesses)
adults.option_b: His miraculous powers and signs
adults.option_c: His education and credentials
adults.option_d: His many converts and churches
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:30 — "If I must needs glory, I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities."

adults.question: What happened to Paul in Damascus?
adults.option_a: The governor under Aretas the king kept the city with a garrison to apprehend him, and through a window in a basket was he let down by the wall and escaped
adults.option_b: He was publicly honoured by the governor
adults.option_c: He established the largest church in Syria
adults.option_d: He debated Greek philosophers in the marketplace
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 11:32-33 — "In Damascus the governor under Aretas the king kept the city of the Damascenes with a garrison, desirous to apprehend me: And through a window in a basket was I let down by the wall, and escaped his hands."

kids.question: Where was Paul caught up to in a vision?
kids.option_a: The third heaven (paradise)
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:2 — "I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago... such an one caught up to the third heaven."

kids.question: What did God say when Paul asked for his problem to be removed?
kids.option_a: My grace is sufficient for thee
kids.option_b: I will take it away right now
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:9 — "And he said unto me, My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness."

kids.question: When is Paul strong?
kids.option_a: When he is weak — for God's power works best in weakness
kids.option_d: When he has lots of money
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:10 — "For when I am weak, then am I strong."

teens.question: What did Paul hear in paradise?
teens.option_a: Unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter
teens.option_b: A list of future events
teens.option_c: The names of all the saved
teens.option_d: Instructions for building a church
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:4 — "How that he was caught up into paradise, and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter."

teens.question: What was the thorn in the flesh given to Paul?
teens.option_a: A messenger of Satan to buffet him, lest he should be exalted above measure through the abundance of revelations
teens.option_b: A physical injury from a shipwreck
teens.option_c: A difficult church member
teens.option_d: A Roman imprisonment
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:7 — "And lest I should be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me, lest I should be exalted above measure."

teens.question: How many times did Paul pray for the thorn to be removed?
teens.option_a: Three times — and the Lord said my grace is sufficient for thee
teens.option_b: Once and God immediately removed it
teens.option_c: Seven times and God finally agreed
teens.option_d: He never prayed about it
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:8 — "For this thing I besought the Lord thrice, that it might depart from me."

teens.question: What does Paul take pleasure in because of Christ?
teens.option_a: Infirmities, reproaches, necessities, persecutions, and distresses — for when I am weak then am I strong
teens.option_b: Wealth, fame, and earthly success
teens.option_c: Comfort, ease, and leisure
teens.option_d: Political power and influence
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:10 — "Therefore I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses for Christ's sake: for when I am weak, then am I strong."

teens.question: What signs proved Paul was truly an apostle?
teens.option_a: The signs of an apostle were wrought among them — signs, wonders, and mighty deeds
teens.option_b: A certificate from the Jerusalem church
teens.option_c: His wealth and social status
teens.option_d: His Roman citizenship
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:12 — "Truly the signs of an apostle were wrought among you in all patience, in signs, and wonders, and mighty deeds."

adults.question: How does Paul describe his heavenly experience?
adults.option_a: A man in Christ caught up to the third heaven fourteen years ago — whether in the body or out of the body he cannot tell, God knoweth
adults.option_b: He had a vivid dream about heaven
adults.option_c: An angel described heaven to him in detail
adults.option_d: He read about heaven in a prophetic scroll
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:2-3 — "I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago, (whether in the body, I cannot tell; or whether out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth;) such an one caught up to the third heaven."

adults.question: Why was the thorn in the flesh given to Paul?
adults.option_a: Lest he should be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations — a messenger of Satan to buffet him
adults.option_b: As punishment for persecuting Christians
adults.option_c: As a test of his physical endurance
adults.option_d: Because he had committed a specific sin
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:7 — "And lest I should be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me."

adults.question: What is the complete statement of God's response about the thorn?
adults.option_a: My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness
adults.option_b: I will remove it when you have suffered enough
adults.option_c: Your faith is not strong enough to overcome it
adults.option_d: This thorn will make you a better preacher
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:9 — "And he said unto me, My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness."

adults.question: What is Paul's response to God's answer about the thorn?
adults.option_a: Most gladly therefore will I rather glory in my infirmities, that the power of Christ may rest upon me
adults.option_b: He was disappointed and frustrated
adults.option_c: He immediately stopped praying entirely
adults.option_d: He sought healing from other sources
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:9 — "Most gladly therefore will I rather glory in my infirmities, that the power of Christ may rest upon me."

adults.question: What does Paul say about what he will not boast of?
adults.option_a: He will glory of his infirmities but of himself he will not glory, except in his infirmities — lest any man should think of him above what he sees or hears
adults.option_b: He will boast freely of all his accomplishments
adults.option_c: He will boast only of his revelations
adults.option_d: He has nothing worth boasting about
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:5-6 — "Of such an one will I glory: yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities. For though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool; for I will say the truth: but now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth of me."

adults.question: What does Paul say about how he treated the Corinthians compared to other churches?
adults.option_a: In what were they inferior to other churches except that Paul himself was not burdensome to them? He asks them to forgive him this wrong
adults.option_b: He treated them more harshly than other churches
adults.option_c: He gave them more money than other churches
adults.option_d: He spent more time with them than any other church
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:13 — "For what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches, except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you? forgive me this wrong."

adults.question: What does Paul say about his upcoming third visit?
adults.option_a: He will not be burdensome to them, for he seeks not theirs but them — the children ought not to lay up for the parents but the parents for the children
adults.option_b: He plans to collect a large offering
adults.option_c: He will demand payment for his preaching
adults.option_d: He expects them to provide luxury accommodations
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:14 — "Behold, the third time I am ready to come to you; and I will not be burdensome to you: for I seek not yours, but you: for the children ought not to lay up for the parents, but the parents for the children."

adults.question: What does Paul fear he may find when he visits Corinth?
adults.option_a: Debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, and tumults — and that God will humble him and he will bewail many who have sinned and not repented
adults.option_b: A perfect and sinless church
adults.option_c: A church that has grown very wealthy
adults.option_d: A church that has forgotten his name
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 12:20-21 — "For I fear, lest, when I come, I shall not find you such as I would... lest there be debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, tumults."

kids.question: What does Paul tell the Corinthians to do to check their faith?
kids.option_a: Examine yourselves whether ye be in the faith
kids.option_b: Ask a priest to check for them
kids.option_c: Read a special book about it
kids.option_d: Wait for a sign from heaven
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:5 — "Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves."

kids.question: What does Paul want the Corinthians to be?
kids.option_a: Perfect (complete) — be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace
kids.option_b: The richest church in the world
kids.option_c: The biggest church in the world
kids.option_d: The most famous church in the world
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:11 — "Finally, brethren, farewell. Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace."

kids.question: Who does Paul say will be with believers?
kids.option_a: The God of love and peace
kids.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:11 — "And the God of love and peace shall be with you."

teens.question: What Old Testament principle does Paul invoke about witnesses?
teens.option_a: In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established
teens.option_b: One witness is sufficient for all matters
teens.option_c: No witnesses are needed if the apostle is present
teens.option_d: Only written testimony is valid
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:1 — "In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established." This quotes Deuteronomy 19:15.

teens.question: What does Paul tell the Corinthians to do about their faith?
teens.option_a: Examine yourselves whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves — know ye not that Jesus Christ is in you except ye be reprobates?
teens.option_b: Let others examine your faith for you
teens.option_c: Faith cannot be examined or tested
teens.option_d: Only apostles can verify genuine faith
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:5 — "Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves. Know ye not your own selves, how that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates?"

teens.question: What four commands does Paul give in his farewell?
teens.option_a: Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace
teens.option_b: Be wealthy, be powerful, be famous, be feared
teens.option_c: Be silent, be still, be hidden, be separate
teens.option_d: Be strong, be brave, be bold, be loud
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:11 — "Finally, brethren, farewell. Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace."

teens.question: How does Paul tell the Corinthians to greet one another?
teens.option_a: With an holy kiss
teens.option_b: With a formal handshake
teens.option_c: With a bow from a distance
teens.option_d: With a wave of the hand
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:12 — "Greet one another with an holy kiss."

teens.question: What famous blessing does Paul give at the end of this letter?
teens.option_a: The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all
teens.option_b: May the force be with you
teens.option_c: Peace and prosperity to all
teens.option_d: May your wealth increase forever
teens.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:14 — "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all. Amen."

adults.question: What warning does Paul give about his third visit?
adults.option_a: He will not spare — if he comes again he will not spare those who have sinned, since they seek a proof of Christ speaking in him
adults.option_b: He will be gentle and overlook all sins
adults.option_c: He will not visit if they don't repent first
adults.option_d: He will send Titus in his place
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:2 — "I told you before, and foretell you, as if I were present, the second time; and being absent now I write to them which heretofore have sinned, and to all other, that, if I come again, I will not spare."

adults.question: What does Paul say about Christ's apparent weakness and power?
adults.option_a: Though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God; we also are weak in him but shall live with him by the power of God toward you
adults.option_b: Christ was never truly weak
adults.option_c: Christ's weakness disproves his divinity
adults.option_d: Power and weakness cannot coexist
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:4 — "For though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God. For we also are weak in him, but we shall live with him by the power of God toward you."

adults.question: What does Paul pray for regarding the Corinthians?
adults.option_a: That they do no evil — not that Paul should appear approved, but that they should do that which is honest, though Paul be as reprobates
adults.option_b: That they send him more money
adults.option_c: That they build a larger church building
adults.option_d: That they submit to his authority without question
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:7 — "Now I pray to God that ye do no evil; not that we should appear approved, but that ye should do that which is honest, though we be as reprobates."

adults.question: What does Paul say about doing anything against the truth?
adults.option_a: We can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth
adults.option_b: Truth is relative to each person
adults.option_c: Truth can be suppressed by authority
adults.option_d: Truth is whatever the majority believes
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:8 — "For we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth."

adults.question: When is Paul glad about his own weakness?
adults.option_a: When the Corinthians are strong — he wishes for their perfection (spiritual maturity)
adults.option_b: When it brings him sympathy
adults.option_c: When it excuses him from work
adults.option_d: When it makes others feel better about themselves
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:9 — "For we are glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong: and this also we wish, even your perfection."

adults.question: Why does Paul write these things while absent?
adults.option_a: Lest being present he should use sharpness, according to the power the Lord gave him for edification and not destruction
adults.option_b: Because he is afraid to confront them in person
adults.option_c: Because writing is easier than speaking
adults.option_d: Because the postal service is faster than travel
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:10 — "Therefore I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness, according to the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the Trinitarian benediction in verse 14?
adults.option_a: It is one of the clearest Trinitarian formulas in Scripture — attributing grace to Christ, love to God the Father, and communion (fellowship) to the Holy Ghost as three distinct persons in one blessing
adults.option_b: It is simply a poetic closing with no doctrinal significance
adults.option_c: It mentions three attributes of one divine person
adults.option_d: It refers to three separate gods working together
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:14 — "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all. Amen." This Trinitarian benediction distinctly names all three persons of the Godhead.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "examine yourselves whether ye be in the faith"?
adults.option_a: Believers should conduct honest self-examination to test whether their faith is genuine — not presuming salvation but verifying that Christ is truly in them
adults.option_b: Only church leaders should examine their faith
adults.option_c: Self-examination is a sign of weak faith
adults.option_d: Examination means checking others' faith
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 13:5 — "Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves. Know ye not your own selves, how that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates?"
